original_abstract:
Do parties actually listen to their voters? While most research on political representation focuses on whether parties align with the ideological positions of the electorate, this article explores an equally vital question: do parties respond to the policy issues that voters prioritize? 

We argue that while parties do reflect voter concerns in their election manifestos, this responsiveness is conditioned by a party's specific characteristics. Specifically, our theory suggests that large "catch-all" parties are the most attuned to shifts in public priorities, whereas governing parties are less responsive, often constrained by their past policy commitments and the scrutiny of office. Furthermore, we find that while niche parties (such as Green or far-right parties) are not more responsive to the general public than mainstream parties, they are significantly more attentive to the priorities of their own core supporters within their specialized issue areas.

To test these arguments, we utilize an extensive dataset combining the Comparative Manifestos Project with voter priority data from the Comparative Study of Electoral Systems and various national election studies. Our analysis covers 130 parties across 18 European democracies and 63 elections between 1972 and 2011. The findings offer new insights into the mechanisms of democratic linkage, demonstrating that the health of political representation depends heavily on which parties are competing for power.
humanized_abstract:
Do parties really listen to citizens? While virtually all the research in the field of political representation is dedicated to understanding whether parties embrace the policy preferences and attitudes of the masses, in this article the more frequently overlooked but arguably equally if not more important “listening to priorities” dimension is analysed.

We examine the degree to which parties reflect the policy preferences of citizens in their election programmes and show that party characteristics condition this relationship. In particular, large, catch-all parties tend to adapt their programmes more to the policy preferences of citizens than other types of parties. We show that ruling parties have lower levels of programme congruence with citizens than opposition parties because of the constraints associated with governing an office. While small parties such as Green or far-right parties are not more congruent with the citizens in general than other parties, they tend to be more congruent with their own citizenry at the level of specific policy issues.

In this paper we investigate the idea of representative democracy where voters delegate to politicians to act as their proxy over time. We test several assumptions implied by this theoretical framework using a rich empirical dataset drawn from the literature on party positions (Comparative Manifestos Project), voter priorities (the Comparative Study of Electoral Systems, CSES, and national electoral studies) and characterizing political representation across different contexts. Our research design involves 130 parties operating within 18 European countries over 63 electoral contests held between 1972 and 2011. As in past research employing comparative data, it turns out that the overall process of political representation in Europe's consensual democracies appears to be strong; yet we also found substantial variation in the performance of individual parties—variation with potential for significant democratic import. These results constitute to the best of our knowledge a completely novel contribution to the literature on the nexus of democracy and delegation: they shed light on the machinery underlying processes of representation, and highlight not only the mechanisms of representative governance but also the factors that contribute to successful political representation in a constitutional democracy, with far-reaching theoretical, empirical and practical implications.
